title: Why Your Snowflake Joins Are Slow: Fix OR Joins Fast
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-26, Reading time: 12 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.greybeam.ai%2Fblog%2Fwhy-your-joins-are-slow%3Futm_source=tldrdata/1/0100019d299ee2b7-1c910ad2-a037-4d9c-aec8-7615d2384dc7-000000/_oIeNixHQwhmYzw7L7vvLxqv5HlDTmP3-NTqcBuwZjY=450
summary: Why Your Snowflake Joins Are Slow: Fix OR Joins Fast (12 minute read) Disjunctive joins (using OR in join conditions) break Snowflake's hash join optimization, forcing expensive Cartesian products and massive performance slowdowns. The fix is to rewrite them as separate equi-joins, restoring efficient execution and often delivering 100–200x speedups.

title: When upserts don't update but still write: Debugging Postgres performance at scale
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-26, Reading time: 11 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.datadoghq.com%2Fblog%2Fengineering%2Fdebugging-postgres-performance%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdata/1/0100019d299ee2b7-1c910ad2-a037-4d9c-aec8-7615d2384dc7-000000/ngx90QKq8yDT95OmeU_LoeCefP61WCAObe3dN1zj-qc=450
summary: When upserts don't update but still write: Debugging Postgres performance at scale (11 minute read) Datadog hit a surprising Postgres performance issue while cleaning up millions of ephemeral hosts: a simple upsert to update the "last seen" timestamp doubled disk writes and quadrupled WAL syncs. The cause was ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE always acquires a row lock and writes to the WAL, even when no data actually changes. The fix is to avoid locking on no-op upserts.

summary: How Anthropic's Claude Thinks (15 minute read) Claude was trained on data, and it developed its own strategies, so the people who built it didn't really know how it worked. title: Google bumps up Q Day deadline to 2029, far sooner than previously thought
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-26, Reading time: 5 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Farstechnica.com%2Fsecurity%2F2026%2F03%2Fgoogle-bumps-up-q-day-estimate-to-2029-far-sooner-than-previously-thought%2F%3Futm_source=tldrnewsletter/1/0100019d29aec9d6-abb4feed-7768-4ae8-be15-1e8bb30546e8-000000/k39HpbKITtR1rWYYW7SCpjr6aXRNQOt06OJIrMB88d0=450
summary: Google bumps up Q Day deadline to 2029, far sooner than previously thought (5 minute read) Q Day is the point at which existing quantum computers can break public-key cryptography algorithms. Everyone should prepare by adopting post-quantum cryptography (PQC) algorithms. Google is now giving itself until 2029 to prepare for the event. The beta version of Android 17 will add support for developers to have PQC keys for signing their apps and verifying other software signatures.

title: Why I Love the Constraints of Email
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-26, Reading time: 5 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Flinks.tldrnewsletter.com%2FFE8wTJ/1/0100019d29d44d1b-85113378-00e9-4d25-b2aa-6cd65c0c4a25-000000/A89s7DcG8nkXc3ep7pjNhtcitXiAJqQxwYx-BZ8sGes=450
summary: Why I Love the Constraints of Email (5 minute read) Email's power comes from its constraints. A narrow 600px layout, outdated HTML, and unpredictable rendering force focus on clarity, strong copy, and intentional design instead of flashy features. These limits reduce decision fatigue and spark creativity, pushing marketers to prioritize the most important message. With fewer distractions and a defined structure, emails deliver higher ROI and conversions than more technically capable channels.

title: Thoughts on slowing the fuck down
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-26, Reading time: 13 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fmariozechner.at%2Fposts%2F2026-03-25-thoughts-on-slowing-the-fuck-down%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019d29d642b0-fb41f7a2-8263-4d3f-98a4-9d67872e5c16-000000/OT7n9R-sYzm3JUhEw7FQ-TDL6I_gHzXeo3sn0THN-QU=450
summary: Thoughts on slowing the fuck down (13 minute read) Using AI coding agents too much leads to brittle, broken, and unmaintainable codebases. Agents compound errors without learning, lack human bottlenecks, generate excessive complexity through local decisions, and suffer from low recall. Instead, humans should slow down and delegate tasks smartly to agents.

summary: Missile Defense is NP-Complete (12 minute read) Missile defense is a complex resource allocation problem where the probability of success is reduced by factors like detection and tracking that adversaries can actively degrade. title: Don't Design for Average Users
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-27, Reading time: 19 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fjakobnielsenphd.substack.com%2Fp%2Fp50-vs-p95%3Futm_source=tldrdesign/1/0100019d2f485297-2447cf69-89db-4791-87e7-55fcec82f08e-000000/v6fXQw0Wl-CuctzUu6e78YLb0mN23MSkfjsUAnfjr2k=450
summary: Don't Design for Average Users (19 minute read) Designing for the average user is a mistake because digital behavior follows skewed distributions rather than normal bell curves. Most users are either casual "tourists" with minimal engagement or ultra-engaged "whale" users in the 95th-99th percentile who generate disproportionate business value. Companies should design for the radical asymmetry between median (P50) and high-end (P95) users rather than optimizing for phantom averages.

title: Why UX Strategy Fails Before the Designer Opens Figma
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-27, Reading time: 9 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.criticalhit.net%2Ftechnology%2Fwhy-ux-strategy-fails-before-the-designer-opens-figma%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdesign/1/0100019d2f485297-2447cf69-89db-4791-87e7-55fcec82f08e-000000/8pfksAL6G-j58goLRJunjOb7W4hDXA1TjhJJ0sD5w58=450
summary: Why UX Strategy Fails Before the Designer Opens Figma (9 minute read) Most UX problems stem from flawed product strategy and untested assumptions rather than poor interface design. Real UX strategy requires thorough user research, precise problem definition, and careful prioritization before any visual design begins. Companies often build products on unexamined assumptions that calcify over time, leading to design solutions that treat only symptoms rather than root causes.

summary: SaaS Was About Unbundling. AI Is About Rebundling (4 minute read) The SaaS playbook was simple - own one workflow, expand from there. Models change every 42 days now, and buyers would rather trust one platform for 3-5 years than stitch together a best-of-breed stack that's outdated by next quarter.

title: Why UUID Primary Keys Quietly Destroy Database Performance
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-30, Reading time: 5 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fankit-rana.com%2Flogs%2F12-uuids-primary-keys-performance%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdata/1/0100019d3e37bd0a-6238a8d3-3ff6-4053-84c3-c9d7bcee95de-000000/X8UxWC6RrN18H_9CwSxG0pX-194sn93TKFmIznMhJ8M=450
summary: Why UUID Primary Keys Quietly Destroy Database Performance (5 minute read) Random UUIDv4 primary keys hurt database performance by causing random inserts, frequent page splits, fragmentation, and poor cache efficiency in B+ tree storage engines, while time-ordered IDs (like UUIDv7/ULID) or using a sequential internal key with a UUID secondary index restore sequential writes and significantly improve efficiency.

title: Pretext
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-30, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fgithub.com%2Fchenglou%2Fpretext%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019d3e869300-4f938c11-6593-44e4-96f4-e025d8f56e2f-000000/3O3ccXM6-hItC9yN-xshop8jIz3BHpCeYs62gJIZB-g=450
summary: Pretext (GitHub Repo) Pretext is a pure JavaScript/TypeScript library for fast and accurate multiline text measurement and layout. It circumvents costly DOM reflows by implementing its own text measurement logic using the browser's font engine. The library has two primary use cases: calculating a paragraph's height without DOM interaction for purposes like virtualization, and providing tools to manually lay out text lines for rendering to Canvas, SVG, or server-side.

title: jai
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-30, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fjai.scs.stanford.edu%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019d3e869300-4f938c11-6593-44e4-96f4-e025d8f56e2f-000000/fxag6FHlqe4HmCZ0akBJP1ZBQVqJJXxRsqoCUgvRj_4=450
summary: jai (Website) AI agents are already causing data loss, from wiped home directories to deleted files, when granted ordinary machine access. jai provides an effortless, one-command solution for containing these agents on Linux, bridging the gap between full system access and complex virtualization. It works by giving your working directory full access while protecting your home directory with a copy-on-write overlay or complete isolation.

title: The Creative Infinite
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-30, Reading time: 4 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fbradfrost.com%2Fblog%2Fpost%2Fthe-creative-infinite%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdesign/1/0100019d3ea443cd-090064af-6fef-4c94-9b6c-f60ec719751e-000000/DnUKa5K-TvlBda-OTDJrNNWvMlDY-mBabNlNq5Ifsds=450
summary: The Creative Infinite (4 minute read) AI represents "the Creative Infinite" - an unprecedented ability to have something exist within minutes of its description. This technology has completely eroded traditional barriers to creation, allowing people to translate their natural thoughts directly into creative outputs. Rather than replacing existing creative tools, AI compounds with them to exponentially expand human creative potential in ways we haven't fully grasped yet.
